COMMONWEALTH NATIONALITY AND CITIZENSHIP

Important changes in the system of nationality law throughout the British Commonwealth have been made as a result of legislation passed by the United Kingdom and the other Members of the Commonwealth during the last few years.

(2) Until 1946 the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand and the Union of South Africa all had statutes which defined who were British subjects and the conditions on which the status of a British subject could be acquired or lost. With some exceptions all these statutes were mutatis mutandis identical. The position of Eire (now the Irish Republic) was exceptional and is dealt with in paragraph (13) below, but in other parts of the Commonwealth, including India, Newfoundland, Southern Rhodesia, and Ceylon, the United Kingdom statutes applied. Thus all British subjects had a common status recognized by the statutes of the several Commonwealth countries, which may be described for convenience as the "common code."

(3) It was a principle of the common code system, affirmed at Imperial Conferences, that alterations to it should be made only as a result of consultation and agreement, but it was recognized that this did not preclude any Member of the Commonwealth from defining by law its own nationals or citizens, though it was important that all such persons should possess the common status. Canada and the Union of South Africa passed such legislation defining their nationals. The Irish Free State also had passed legislation defining its citizens, but the legislation was so framed that these persons did not possess the status of British subjects. No other Member of the Commonwealth had passed legislation defining its own nationals or citizens.

(4) In 1946, Canada, after giving notice to the other Members of the Commonwealth, passed legislation (the Canadian Citizenship Act) which was framed on a different basis. It defined the persons who would be Canadian citizens, enacted that all Canadian citizens were British subjects and recognized as British subjects in Canada persons who were British subjects under the law of the part of the Commonwealth to which they belonged. Thus the Canadian Act continued to recognize the common status, while departing from the common code system.

(5) The United Kingdom Government felt that the question should be considered whether this form of legislation could not with advantage be adopted generally throughout the Commonwealth. Apart from the desirability that the laws of the different countries on a question of general Commonwealth concern such as nationality should be consistent with one another, it was considered that adoption of the Canadian plan by all Members of the Commonwealth, including this country, would have certain administrative advantages. Accordingly a Conference of expert representatives from the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, Eire, Newfoundland, Southern Rhodesia, Burma, and Ceylon met in London, in February, 1947. No representative of India attended but arrangements were made to keep the Government of India informed of the proceedings. Agreement was reached at the Conference on a general scheme, which was recommended by the representatives to their Governments. The essential features of this were that each country would by its legislation determine who were its citizens and (except in the case of Eire) declare these citizens to be British subjects, and recognize as British subjects the citizens of the other countries. For this purpose the United Kingdom and Colonies were to be treated as a unit for citizenship purposes and their citizens called "citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies." As a result of correspondence between the Governments, there was general acceptance of this scheme, and legislation giving effect to it, so far as the United Kingdom was concerned, was passed in the form of the British Nationality Act, 1948.
(6) The general effect of this Act was as follows :

1. It defined what persons would under the law of the United Kingdom have the status of British subjects, viz. , citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, Newfound-
Digitized by Google

land, India, Pakistan, Southern Rhodesia, and Ceylon. In consequence of the subsequent entry of Newfoundland into the Canadian Confederation, the reference to Newfoundland was repealed by the Newfoundland (Consequential Provisions) Act, 1950. Owing to misconception in some Commonwealth countries as to the significance of the term "British subject," it was provided that a person having that status might be known either as a British subject or as a Commonwealth citizen.
2. It defined the conditions on which citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies would in future be acquired or lost.

3. It contained transitional provisions defining the position as regards citizenship of persons who on the date of coming into force of the Act (the 1st January, 1949) were British subjects under the existing law.
4. It contained special provisions as to the position of citizens of Eire, and of British-protected persons.
(7) The principal qualifications whereby citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies is acquired under the Act are :*
(a) Birth in the United Kingdom † or a Colony.
(b) Descent

(i) if the person's father was a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies and was so by birth, registration, or naturalization, or was in United Kingdom Government service at the time of the birth ; or

(ii) if the person's father was a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies by descent and the birth took place in a Protectorate, Protected State or Trust Territory or, having taken place in a foreign country, was registered at a United Kingdom Consulate.
(c) Registration as a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies :

(i) in the case of a citizen of another Commonwealth country or of the Irish Republic ordinarily resident for one year in the United Kingdom or a Colony, or in United Kingdom Government service at the time ;
(ii) in the case of a woman of any citizenship or nationality who has been married to a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies ( see paragraph 9 below) ;
(iii) in the case of a minor of any citizenship or nationality.
(d) Naturalization in the case of an alien fulfilling certain conditions as to residence in the British Commonwealth and otherwise.
(8) The circumstances in which citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies can be lost are, in general :
(i) a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies possessing the citizenship or nationality of any other country can voluntarily renounce his citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies ;
(ii) a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies by registration ( see paragraph 7 (c)) can be deprived of such citizenship if registration has been obtained by fraud or false representation ;

(iii) a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies by naturalization ( see paragraph 7 (d)) can be deprived of such citizenship not only if naturalization has been obtained by fraud or false representation but also on certain specified grounds ( e.g. , disloyalty, trading with the enemy, imprisonment, lengthy residence in foreign countries).
An opportunity has to be given of being heard by a Committee of Enquiry before action is taken.
* Only a general statement of the law is given in the article and reference should be made to the Acts themselves for any particular case.

† The expression "the United Kingdom" is used throughout in the sense of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland. The Channel Islands and the Isle of Man were dealt with in the Act in the same way as Colonies.
Digitized by Google

(9) In these respects the conditions for acquisition and loss of citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies correspond substantially to those prescribed by the existing law for acquisition and loss of British Nationality. There was, however, one important change made by the Act, viz. , as regards the nationality of married women. Hitherto the position had been that the nationality of a married woman depended on that of her husband. Thus an alien woman who married a British subject became ipso facto a British subject. A British woman who married an alien became an alien unless, under the law of her husband's country, she did not acquire the nationality of that country as a result of the marriage. The effect of the 1948 Act is to place a married woman, for nationality purposes, in the position of a single woman. Consequently,
(a) a woman who is a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies does not lose her citizenship and the status of a British subject by marrying an alien ;
(b) a woman who had already lost British nationality by marriage to an alien automatically re-acquired it on the coming into force of the Act ;

(c) an alien woman marrying a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies does not now automatically acquire citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies but is entitled, at her wish, to be registered as such a citizen on taking an Oath of Allegiance ;

(d) a woman who is a citizen of another Commonwealth country or of the Irish Republic marrying a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies is similarly entitled to be registered, and does not have to take an Oath of Allegiance.

(10) As stated in paragraph 6 (3), the Act contained transitional provisions as to the citizenship of persons already living when the Act came into force who were British subjects. The object was twofold :
(1) To select from the whole body of British subjects those who would automatically become citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies.

(2) To ensure that other British subjects whose connection with another Commonwealth country was such that they were likely to become citizens of that country under its legislation when passed would not lose their British nationality either during the interval before that legislation was passed or after it had been passed if they did not in fact become citizens of the other country concerned.
(11) As regards (1) above, the British subjects who became automatically citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies on the 1st January, 1949, were, in general :
(a) those who were born in the United Kingdom or a Colony, Protectorate, Protected State, or United Kingdom Trust Territory ;
(b) those who had been naturalized in the United Kingdom or a Colony ;
(c) the wives of citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies ;
(d) those born outside the United Kingdom and Colonies whose fathers were born or naturalized in the United Kingdom or a Colony ;

(e) other persons who derived British nationality from a more remote ancestor, e.g. , a grandfather born in the United Kingdom, and who had no nearer connection with another Commonwealth country ( e.g. , by their own birth in such a country).

(12) As regards (2) in paragraph 10, the Act provided that the persons concerned, i.e. , those who might eventually be declared by the legislation of another Commonwealth country to be its citizens (described in the Act as "potential citizens" of such a country), would, pending the passing of such legislation, be "British Subjects without citizenship." This provision covers, for example, a person born in a Commonwealth country such as British India, whose father also was born in that country, though his grandfather was born in the United Kingdom, or a person born in a foreign country whose father was born in Australia. The Act provided that, if
Digitized by Google

eventually, when the other country concerned (India or Australia in the above examples) had passed its own citizenship law, he did not become a citizen of that country, he would automatically become a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies and would thus retain his British nationality throughout. In the meantime, he would be able to be registered as a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies, if he fulfilled the conditions mentioned in paragraph 7 (c) (i) above (ordinary residence of a year in the United Kingdom or a Colony or United Kingdom Government service). Special provision was also made for existing British subjects not fulfilling those conditions who, by ancestry and close association were connected with, and intended to make their ordinary place of residence in, the United Kingdom or a Colony, e.g. , members of the European community in India and Pakistan who did not automatically become citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies under the provisions mentioned in paragraph 11. They were enabled to be registered, at the discretion of the United Kingdom authorities, as citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies provided that their applications were submitted before the 1st January, 1950. Some 3,000 persons have been so registered in India and Pakistan, and there were a few cases in other Commonwealth countries.

(13) As was stated in paragraphs 2 and 3, the position of Eire in this matter had for some years been a special one. As a result of legislation passed by the Irish Free State in 1935, Eire citizens were not British subjects under Eire law, though with a few exceptions they were still British subjects under United Kingdom law. This situation was both anomalous in itself and the cause of some complaint by the Eire Government, though they were ready to agree to reciprocal arrangements under which citizens of one of the two countries would be accorded by the Government of the other privileges not generally granted to nationals of foreign States, e.g. , as regards entry and residence.

(14) Accordingly the 1948 Act was so framed that in future Eire citizens* would not be British subjects under United Kingdom law, unless they possessed that status for some reason other than connection with Eire; for example, a person born in Eire whose father was born in the United Kingdom is a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies and therefore a British subject, see paragraph 7 (b) (i) above.

(15) It was, however, recognized that there were a number of Eire citizens having associations of one kind or another with the United Kingdom who would resent being deprived of their status as British subjects against their will. Accordingly provision was inserted in the Act enabling such persons to retain their status as British subjects under United Kingdom law by giving notice to the United Kingdom Government of their desire to do so. Some 20,000 persons have taken advantage of this provision. It should be noted that their status is that of British subjects only and not that of citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies. Consequently children of such persons born after the 1st January, 1949, are not British subjects unless they acquire that status in some other way, e.g. , by birth in the United Kingdom or by registration as citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies as mentioned in paragraph 7 (c) above. This provision applies only to persons who were Eire citizens under Eire law on the 1st January, 1949; persons connected with Eire who were not such citizens ( e.g. , those born in Ireland but not domiciled there when the Irish Free State was established) became citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies unless they were citizens or potentially citizens of another Commonwealth country.

(16) In order to give effect to the desire of the Eire Government for reciprocal arrangements as to grant of privileges (which would be of practical advantage to the United Kingdom also) it is further provided in the Act that Eire citizens who were not British subjects would continue to be treated in the United Kingdom and the Colonies in the same way as British subjects unless and until alteration was made in the law. As regards acquisition of citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies the Act applies to them in the same way as it applies to citizens of
* The provisions of the 1948 Act relating to Eire citizens now apply to citizens of the Irish Republic.
Digitized by Google

Commonwealth countries other than the United Kingdom and Colonies. Thus, those citizens are entitled to all the rights accorded to British subjects in the United Kingdom, e.g. , entry and residence, the practice of professions and the franchise, on the same conditions as British subjects. Citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies enjoy certain reciprocal rights in the Irish Republic, especially as regards entry and residence.

(17) The 1948 Act also contains special provisions dealing with British protected persons , i.e. , those belonging to Protectorates, Protected States and Trust Territories. These persons under existing law were not British subjects and consequently were in law aliens. The Act does not confer upon them the status of citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies or British subjects, but places them in some respects in a more favourable position than foreign nationals. Thus, it is provided that the Aliens Restriction Acts will not apply to protected persons, and that protected persons may be naturalized as citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies on easier terms than nationals of foreign States.

(18) As was mentioned in paragraph 4, legislation on the general lines of the recommendations of the 1947 Expert Conference had already been passed by Canada in 1946. Since the passing of the United Kingdom Act of 1948, similar legislation has been passed by all the other Commonwealth countries concerned except India (Australia, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, Pakistan, Ceylon and Southern Rhodesia), and Canada has amended her Act so as to bring the provision about the common status into line with the United Kingdom Act. The legislation, for the most part, corresponds closely with that of the United Kingdom, but there are various differences, some of which are important from the point of view of this country or generally.

(19) While the legislation of Canada , Australia , New Zealand and Southern Rhodesia provides in terms that citizens of the country concerned and of all other Commonwealth countries are British subjects, the legislation of the Union of South Africa contains no similar provision and consequently does not provide for the maintenance of a "common status" as that term has hitherto been understood. In this case, however, it is provided that citizens of other Commonwealth countries (and of the Irish Republic) are not "aliens" for the purposes of the Act, and such persons are in some respects accorded more favourable treatment than nationals of foreign States, e.g. , as regards the period of residence required before registration as citizens of South Africa ( see paragraph 21 below). The Pakistan Act provides that citizens of Pakistan are Commonwealth citizens but does not provide in terms that citizens of other Commonwealth countries possess the same status, though it contains provisions that the expression "Commonwealth citizen" in the Act means a person described as such in the British Nationality Act, 1948, and that a Commonwealth citizen is not an alien for the purposes of the Pakistan Act. Ceylon similarly has not made any provision for "common status" though it is understood that further legislation on this subject is contemplated. In all these cases, however, citizens of the countries concerned still possess the status of British subjects (or Commonwealth citizens) under United Kingdom law.

(20) There is also a considerable difference between the practical effects of the possession of the common status in the various Commonwealth countries. In the United Kingdom no distinction is drawn, as regards the enjoyment of rights, between citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies and citizens of other Commonwealth countries, and such distinctions as are drawn are between British subjects (or Commonwealth citizens) and aliens. Thus, all British subjects are entitled to enter the United Kingdom without restriction and cannot, like aliens, be deported, even if they are citizens of another Commonwealth country and not citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies. Again all British subjects enjoy the franchise on the same conditions and can be members of either House of Parliament or of the Privy Council, whereas an alien is not entitled to any of these privileges. Similarly admission to certain professions such as the Civil Service and that of solicitor is restricted to British subjects and is not open to aliens. In other Commonwealth
Digitized by Google

countries the rights possessed by British subjects who are not citizens of the country concerned are, to a varying extent, more limited. All these countries impose restrictions on the immigration of persons not possessing their citizenship and possess and sometimes exercise the right to deport such persons after admission, though in practice in most cases the conditions on which British subjects can enter these countries are less onerous than those imposed on aliens. In Canada, Australia and New Zealand all British subjects are entitled to the franchise on the same terms, but in the other countries the franchise is limited to citizens of the country. Admission to the professions is in general open to all British subjects whether citizens of the country concerned or not.

(21) In several instances also the conditions on which citizenship of the country can be acquired by persons not already in possession of it are easier in the case of citizens of other Commonwealth countries than in the case of aliens. In the United Kingdom , as was stated in paragraph 7 (c) (i), one year's ordinary residence is sufficient to qualify for acquisition of citizenship by registration, whereas an alien can only be naturalized if he has in addition been resident in the United Kingdom or the Colonial Empire for four years during the previous seven. The position is similar in New Zealand , though in that case, registration is not, as in the United Kingdom, automatic but is at the discretion of the Minister. In the Union of South Africa a citizen of a Commonwealth country may, at the discretion of the Minister, be registered as a South African citizen after five years' residence, whereas an alien requires six years' residence (and moreover must, unlike a citizen of a Commonwealth country, take an Oath of Allegiance). In Southern Rhodesia the qualifying period of residence is three years for citizens of Commonwealth countries as against five years, as in this country, for an alien; there are other conditions which are substantially the same as those for aliens, but if these are fulfilled, registration is of right. In Canada and Australia , on the other hand, the conditions generally, including the period of residence, are the same for citizens of other Commonwealth countries and aliens, and registration is discretionary, though the Australian Act provides that all British subjects (not otherwise Australian citizens) who had been ordinarily resident in Australia for five years at the date of coming into force of the Act automatically became Australian citizens. In Pakistan the conditions as to registration are not specified in the Act, but are to be subsequently determined by the Government. In Ceylon the categories of person who can acquire Ceylon citizenship by registration are limited, and no distinction is drawn between citizens of Commonwealth countries and aliens.

(22) There are also some differences between the legislation of the various countries in so far as they permit dual citizenship within the Commonwealth. Such citizenship may be acquired in one of two ways, (a) automatically, e.g. , by a person born in one country whose father was born in another, or (b) by voluntary acquisition as a result of registration. Dual citizenship may be of practical advantage to its possessor, since, if he is at the time resident in a country which grants certain privileges ( e.g. , the franchise) to its own citizens only, he can obtain those privileges without having to lose the citizenship of his second country where he may later become a resident or a visitor. It does not, however, enable its possessor to claim, in relation to the Government of the country where he is at the time, the protection of the Government of his second country.

(23) So far as concerns persons automatically possessing citizenship of two or more Commonwealth countries, the United Kingdom law provides, as mentioned in paragraph 8 (1), that a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies who is also a national or citizen of another country can renounce his citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies if he so desires, but it does not require him to do so. The position is generally similar in the other Commonwealth countries, but the laws of Pakistan and Ceylon provide that a citizen of the country who is also a national or citizen of another country must either renounce that citizenship or forfeit Pakistan or Ceylon citizenship as the case may be, i.e. , he cannot possess dual citizenship.
Digitized by Google
(In the case of Pakistan this provision appears to be of temporary application only except in the case of minors.)

(24) As regards citizens of one Commonwealth country who acquire citizenship of another by registration, the position in the United Kingdom is as described in the previous paragraph, i.e. , a person who becomes a citizen of another Commonwealth country by registration does not lose his citizenship of the United Kingdom and Colonies unless he chooses voluntarily to renounce it. The position is the same in New Zealand and Southern Rhodesia . On the other hand, Canada , Australia , the Union of South Africa and Ceylon provide that any of their citizens who voluntarily acquire citizenship of another country thereby lose their original citizenship. Pakistan also, as stated in the previous paragraph, does not permit its citizens to possess dual citizenship, at any rate as regards the immediate future. Ceylon provides also that any person registered as a Ceylon citizen must renounce his former citizenship unless exempted from doing so by the Minister. To this extent some Commonwealth countries restrict the possession of dual citizenship.

(25) The position as regards the citizenship of persons born in foreign countries also is not uniform. As stated in paragraph 7 (b) (i) and (ii) United Kingdom law provides that a person born in a foreign country, whose father was born or naturalized or registered in the United Kingdom or a Colony, is automatically a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies, and that children of subsequent generations acquire the same status provided that their birth has been registered at a United Kingdom Consulate. As regards persons born after the coming into force of the relevant legislation, the position is substantially similar in the case of Australia , New Zealand , the Union of South Africa , Pakistan and Ceylon , except that Australia, the Union and Ceylon require registration of the birth even in the case of the first generation. Canada provides solely for children of the first generation and then only if the birth is registered, and the position is generally the same in the case of Southern Rhodesia . In the case of persons born before the coming into force of the new legislation, however, it is not provided in all cases that persons born in foreign countries who derived their British nationality solely from descent from an ancestor belonging to the country concerned acquire the citizenship of that country. Where this is not provided, the persons become citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies as a result of the provisions mentioned in paragraph 12 and can transmit that status to their descendants, provided that the appropriate conditions are fulfilled.

(26) There are various other differences of detail which need not be particularized. While, however, the new system creates a certain amount of complication and even some anomalies, it has two main advantages. From the practical point of view, the fact that each of the countries of the British Commonwealth has now defined by law its own citizens is of great assistance in administrative matters such as the drafting of treaties and of domestic legislation, the issue of passports, and the protection of individuals in foreign countries, since it is now possible and usually easy, as was not formerly the case, to determine in any individual instance to which country of the Commonwealth a given person is to be regarded as belonging. From the more general standpoint, the fact that all the Members of the Commonwealth, while establishing their own separate citizenships, continue to differentiate, in greater or less degree, between citizens of Commonwealth countries and aliens is a characteristic illustration of the nature of the Commonwealth as it exists today, combining as it does the sovereign independence of each of its Members with a special relationship between them different from, and far more intimate than, that existing between countries foreign to one another.

OVERSEA SETTLEMENT

Migration from the United Kingdom to other parts of the Empire during the ten years 1904-1913 averaged over 148,000 migrants annually. The four years immediately before the 1914-1918 war saw the peak of this movement, and in 1913 over 285,000 migrants proceeded overseas to other parts of the Empire. This movement took place without any assistance from the United Kingdom Government, except for an Emigrants' Information Office which was set up to provide information to intending migrants. In 1917 the Dominions Royal Commission in its Final Report (Cd. 8462) suggested that the United Kingdom Government should exercise a greater measure of control over emigration and with that object should establish a Central Emigration Authority, as an independent Department of the United Kingdom Government, assisted by a Consultative Board. As a result, an Emigration Bill providing for the establishment of a statutory authority was introduced into Parliament in 1918, but this Bill met with opposition and was withdrawn. It was, however, decided that the Secretary of State for the Colonies should appoint a Government Emigration Committee "in view of the need for creating machinery to enable His Majesty's Government to deal effectively with the emigration problems which are likely to arise during the period of reconstruction after the war." This Committee, renamed in 1919 the Oversea Settlement Committee, absorbed the functions of the Emigrants' Information Office, and its staff formed a sub-department of the Colonial Office.

Apart from the provision of authentic information for men and women who after war service were thinking of settling overseas, the first task of the Committee was to draw up a memorandum on the whole subject of migration policy which contained, inter alia , the following recommendations :

(a) That the migration of population from the United Kingdom to other parts of the Empire was calculated to promote the economic strength and the well-being of the Empire as a whole, and of the United Kingdom in particular, provided that the flow at any time was not in excess of what the industries of the United Kingdom could afford to spare or in excess of what the Dominions could conveniently absorb.

(b) That there was at that time no sufficient reason for the direct grant of State aid to emigration in general, beyond the aid involved in the improvement and cheapening of inter-Imperial communications.

(c) That, as a reward for their services in the 1914-1918 war, ex-servicemen who wished to go to other parts of the Empire and were either bona fide settlers on the land or had definite offers of other suitable employment should be entitled to a free passage to their destination for themselves and their dependants.

The Committee's recommendation at (c) was substantially accepted by the United Kingdom Government and some 86,027 ex-service men and women and their dependants sailed under the Government Free Passage Scheme of 1919-1922 ; of these, 26,905 went to Canada and 37,576 to Australia.

A Conference on State-Aided Empire Settlement was held in 1921 between representatives of the United Kingdom Government and of the Governments of Canada, the Commonwealth of Australia and New Zealand. The purpose of the Conference was to advise upon an enduring policy of oversea settlement which should tend to bring about the best distribution of manpower and so to develop and strengthen the whole Empire.

The Conference recommended effective co-operation between the United Kingdom and the oversea Governments in a comprehensive policy of Empire land settlement and Empire directed migration extending over a period of years.
The Conference of Prime Ministers and representatives of the United Kingdom,
Digitized by Google
the Dominions and India, held later in 1921, adopted these recommendations and passed the following resolution :

“The Conference having satisfied itself that the proposals embodied in the Report of the Conference on State-Aided Empire Settlement are sound in principle, and that the several Dominions are prepared, subject to Parliamentary sanction and to the necessary financial arrangements being made, to co-operate effectively with the United Kingdom in the development of schemes based on these proposals, but adapted to the particular circumstances and conditions of each Dominion, approves the aforesaid Report.

“The South African representatives wish to make it clear that the limited field for white labour in South Africa will preclude co-operation by the Union Government on the lines contemplated by the other Dominions.

“ (2) The Conference expresses the hope that the Government of the United Kingdom will at the earliest possible moment secure the necessary powers to enable it to carry out its part in any schemes of co-operation which may subsequently be agreed on, preferably in the form of an Act which will make clear that the policy of co-operation now adopted is intended to be permanent.

“ (3) The Conference recommends to the Governments of the several Dominions that they should consider how far their existing legislation on the subject of land settlement, soldier settlement and immigration, may require any modification or expansion in order to secure effective co-operation ; and should work out, for discussion with the Government of the United Kingdom, such proposals as may appear to them most practicable and best suited to their interests and circumstances.”

As a result of this resolution the Empire Settlement Act, 1922 was passed on the 31st May, 1922. Under this Act the Secretary of State was empowered to co-operate with any oversea Government or with public authorities or public or private organizations either in the United Kingdom or in any part of His Majesty’s dominions in carrying out agreed schemes for affording joint assistance to suitable persons in the United Kingdom who intended to settle in any part of His Majesty’s dominions. Agreed schemes under the Act might be either (i) development or land-settlement schemes or (ii) schemes for assisted passages, training, etc. The Act provided that the Secretary of State should not agree to any scheme without the consent of the Treasury, that he should not contribute more than 50 per cent. of the cost of any scheme, and that his liability to contribute should not extend beyond a period of 15 years after the passing of the Act. It also provided that the aggregate amount expended by the Secretary of State on any scheme or schemes should not exceed £3,000,000 in any one financial year exclusive of the amount of any sums received by way of interest on or repayment of advances previously made.

While these plans were being made for encouraging and quickening the redistribution of population throughout the Empire in accordance with the wishes of the oversea Governments, the spontaneous unassisted movement which had practically stopped at the outbreak of war in 1914 had been resumed. Between 1919 and 1922, apart from the 86,000 ex-service men and women and their dependants who sailed under the Free Passage Scheme, some 484,000 migrants proceeded to various parts of the Empire.
The Imperial Economic Conference held in 1923 emphasized its sense of the importance of the policy of oversea settlement to the well-being of the Empire.
The Imperial Conference, 1926, adopted the following Resolution :

“The Conference is of opinion that the problem of oversea settlement, which is that of a redistribution of the white population of the Empire in the best interests of the whole British Commonwealth, is one of paramount importance, especially as between Great Britain on the one hand and Canada, Australia, and New Zealand on the other. The Conference notes with satisfaction that the desired redistribution of population is being accelerated by the policy which has been consistently pursued since its acceptance by the Resolution of the Conference of Prime Ministers in 1921. It endorses the view expressed at that Conference that the policy should be a permanent one. It recognizes that it would be impracticable, owing to financial, economic and political considerations, to promote mass movements of population, but it is satisfied that, by continuous adherence to the present policy, it should be possible steadily to increase the flow of population to those parts of the British Commonwealth where it is most needed for development and general security, and where it will find the greatest opportunities.”
Digitized by Google

During the period 1922-1929 various schemes of assisted passages were operated under agreements with the Governments of Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and Southern Rhodesia. In addition, there were numerous subsidiary plans for the testing and training of prospective migrants, including juveniles and families. Agreements with recognized child migration societies and other voluntary organizations, including Local Migration Committees, provided for assistance towards the cost of passage, outfit, incidental expenses, and aftercare.

As regards schemes of land settlement and development, an agreement was signed in April, 1925, with the Government of the Commonwealth of Australia under which it was intended to raise loans not exceeding in the aggregate £34,000,000 (including loans of £14,000,000 provided for under the land settlement agreements previously arranged with the States of Victoria, New South Wales, and Western Australia). The United Kingdom Government were to provide half the interest on the loans for the first ten years; the Commonwealth Government were to issue the loans to the Governments of the Australian States at 2 per cent. interest for ten years, and the responsibility for the balance of the interest and for the capital amount of the loan was to rest with the Government of the State in which the development or land settlement was to take place. The State Governments however found difficulty in formulating satisfactory schemes, and in the event only some £7,865,000 was actually advanced to them by the Commonwealth Government. The contributions of the United Kingdom Government between 1925 and 1933 amounted to £1,436,005.

In addition to the above-mentioned land-settlement schemes in Australia, schemes with a similar purpose were arranged with the Government of Canada, the New Brunswick Government, the Hudson Bay Company, and the Canadian Pacific Railway Company. A land-settlement scheme was also arranged with the Government of Southern Rhodesia. Contrary to expectations formed when the Empire Settlement Act was introduced, only a small proportion, estimated at 7½ per cent., of assisted migrants proceeded under schemes of planned land settlement. Of the people who settled within the Empire overseas from 1922 to 1931, with and without assistance, planned land-settlement schemes accounted for only 3 per cent. of the total (31,000 out of over 1,000,000).

The schemes operated under the Empire Settlement Act lasted from its inception in 1922 until 1929 when, owing to the world-wide economic depression, migration practically stopped. It is noteworthy that whereas it had been expected that the expenditure under the Act on schemes of land settlement and development would be twice as great as the expenditure on schemes of assisted passages, training, etc., in actual practice the position was reversed, the total expenditure by the United Kingdom Government on land settlement and development schemes amounting to £2,400,000 and on schemes of assisted passages, training, etc., to £4,300,000, whilst the number of assisted migrants proceeding to schemes of land settlement was approximately 30,000 out of a total assisted movement of approximately 404,000.

The deterioration of economic conditions overseas which began to develop during 1929 resulted in a balance inwards from 1930 until 1937, but this was not due to any increase in the number of people coming from overseas but solely to the decrease in the number of those going out.
The Imperial Conference of 1930 reaffirmed overseas settlement policy and the Resolution adopted pointed out that :

“ The Conference recognizes that the economic difficulties of the present time are such as to render impracticable any considerable flow of migrants from the United Kingdom to the Dominions, but it has every confidence in the future and recommends that the problem of overseas settlement should continue to receive the most careful consideration and that the Governments concerned should adopt such measures as may be found best calculated to secure the object in view as and when economic conditions permit.”

In 1934 an Inter-Departmental Committee was appointed to recommend, in the light of past experience, the extent and manner in which, if at all, the United Kingdom should in future encourage and assist migration.
Digitized by Google

In reviewing operations under the Empire Settlement Act, 1922, the Committee found that the total cost to the United Kingdom Government of the various forms of assistance provided during the period 1922 to 1933 had been upwards of £6,000,000. Grants and advances towards the cost of passages to 404,729 persons accounted for £3,851,063 of the total expenditure.

The Committee recommended that the United Kingdom Government should only assist migration when conditions were favourable to satisfactory settlement and oversea Governments were prepared to receive new migrants and join in the arrangements necessary to give them a real prospect of success. Other recommendations concerned the amendment of the Empire Settlement Act, 1922, so as to allow a higher governmental contribution towards the cost of the work of approved voluntary organizations and the discontinuance, except in certain exceptional circumstances, of the policy of providing capital for land settlement.

A further recommendation of the Inter-Departmental Committee was that there should be set up an Oversea Settlement Board with the duty of "considering and advising the Secretary of State upon specific proposals for schemes of migration within the Empire and upon any matter relating to oversea settlement which may be referred to it by him."

The Oversea Settlement Board was appointed in February, 1936, and one of its first tasks was to consider whether the Empire Settlement Act, 1922, should be renewed for a further period beyond the 31st May, 1937, with or without amendment. As a result of a preliminary review of the situation the Board were encouraged by evidence of improving economic conditions overseas and a growing interest in the Dominions, and they recommended the early enactment of legislation to empower the Secretary of State to continue his contributions to existing schemes, such as that for co-operation with Fairbridge Farm Schools in the establishment and maintenance of farm schools for children in Canada and Australia, and to co-operate in agreed schemes similar to those specified in the Act of 1922 when conditions favoured their introduction. Under that Act, expenditure from public funds in any financial year might not exceed £3 millions. In practice, expenditure had never reached even one-half of that amount; the maximum in the peak year (1927) was only £1,280,000, and the average expenditure from 1923 to the beginning of the depression (1929) was rather more than £800,000 per annum. During the years 1933, 1934 and 1935, when migration from the United Kingdom had declined to negligible proportions, the total expenditure on land settlement schemes and passage allowances was £78,000, £39,000 and £31,000 respectively.

In considering the limit of expenditure to be adhered to if the Act of 1922 were to be extended, the Board favoured, on grounds of principle, restriction of the Secretary of State's contribution to a maximum of 50 per cent. when the partner in the scheme was an oversea Government. Where the partner in a scheme was an organization financed otherwise than from Government funds, it was considered that it should be open to the Secretary of State to contribute, if he thought fit, up to 75 per cent. of the cost.

An Act to amend the Empire Settlement Act, 1922, was passed on the 19th March, 1937. This Act, the Empire Settlement Act, 1937, extended the Act of 1922 for a further period of fifteen years to the 31st May, 1952, and while the maximum expenditure to be incurred in any one year was reduced from £3 millions to £1½ millions, it empowered the Secretary of State to "contribute an amount not exceeding three-quarters of the expenses of any such (agreed) scheme except :
(a) a development or land-settlement scheme; or (b) a scheme towards the expenses of which the Government of a part of His Majesty's oversea dominions have agreed to contribute."

The Oversea Settlement Board made a close study of matters affecting Empire migration and settlement and, in a comprehensive report published in June, 1938 (Cmd. 5766), gave their considered views on the more general aspects of migration between the United Kingdom and Canada, the Commonwealth of Australia, New
Digitized by Google

Zealand, and the Union of South Africa. The Board considered that the problem of how to strengthen the Empire was both difficult and urgent and that its solution depended upon the wholehearted co-operation of the Governments and peoples concerned. The problem was also of importance in view of the interest shown throughout the world in the development of the less-closely settled countries. The position of the oversea Dominions in relatively thinly populated areas facing a highly competitive world demanded that the natural increase of population should be supplemented by immigration; and early action should be taken while the United Kingdom was still able to supply migrants. It could no longer be assumed as axiomatic that the migration of large numbers of persons from the United Kingdom to the Dominions was in the interests of the United Kingdom, if those interests could be considered in isolation from those of the Dominions. Even at that time it was only from the point of view of the strengthening of the Empire as a whole that the encouragement of migration from the United Kingdom could be justified. The resumption of migration on a large scale would, unless the existing trends were checked, diminish still further the population of the United Kingdom. In the Board's opinion, however, no merely theoretical calculations of population movements should be allowed to affect migration policy.

Up to the outbreak of war in 1939, economic conditions in the Dominions continued to restrict the number of openings for new settlers, particularly for those dependent on immediate employment for their livelihood, and it was only possible to resume assisted migration on a small scale to Australia and Southern Rhodesia.

A White Paper on Migration within the British Commonwealth, published in July, 1945 (Cmd. 6658), explained that it continued to be the policy of the United Kingdom Government to encourage and assist, as far as might be practicable, inter-Imperial migration. It also explained that the United Kingdom Government had put forward proposals for a free-passage scheme for ex-Service men and women, somewhat similar to that in operation between 1919 and 1922, and that the general question of assisted migration in the immediate post-war period had been under discussion with the Governments of Canada, the Commonwealth of Australia, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, and Southern Rhodesia by correspondence in 1943, at the meeting of Prime Ministers in May, 1944, and in subsequent meetings with Dominion representatives in London. The oversea Governments, however, were at that time occupied with the rehabilitation of their own ex-Service men and women and with other post-war problems, which precluded immediate consideration of any plans for assisting immigration.

The policy of the United Kingdom Government regarding migration to Commonwealth countries has remained unchanged since 1943. It was set forth in the White Paper referred to above and can be summed up in the statement made by the Secretary of State in the House of Commons on the 13th June, 1950 (Hansard, col. 99), in which he said :

“The solidarity and increasing strength of the Commonwealth depends on the increasing migration of people from this country to those countries who wish to receive our migrants. We must take account of the fact that countries like Australia, New Zealand and Canada will in any case be receiving migrants from other than British sources, and it is all the more important that there should be an adequate flow of British migrants to those countries, if they will have them. Our firm policy is to facilitate and to encourage the outflow of all those people who wish to leave these islands, although we cannot compel them to go to the countries who wish to receive them. That is not just a formal policy which we state and re-state. We have in fact been carrying it out with vigour.”

Since the war the United Kingdom and Australian Governments have co-operated in promoting migration from this country, and more than 135,000 persons have been accepted for settlement in Australia under the free and assisted passage schemes which came into operation in 1947. Great efforts have been made by the United Kingdom Government to provide special shipping facilities for migration to Australia from the United Kingdom and, as a result, the movement has been proceeding at a steadily increasing rate. The Australian Government have an
Digitized by Google
ambitious immigration programme and hope that Australia's population may be raised to ten million in 1960 as compared with the present figure of about eight million.

No similar arrangements for passage assistance have been entered into with the Government of any other Commonwealth country, but agreements, also under the Acts, have been made with certain Child Migration Societies which provide for contributions by the Secretary of State towards the cost of the maintenance, etc., of children under the age of 16 years at approved Farm Schools, Homes, and other institutions in Canada, Australia, and Southern Rhodesia. Contributions of this nature have been made in respect of some 1,500 children in the post-war period.
The Canadian Government have made arrangements of their own to encourage the migration from the United Kingdom of as many carefully selected settlers as can advantageously be absorbed.

Since July, 1947, the New Zealand Government have operated, at their own expense, free and assisted passage schemes for young single men and women. This programme was enlarged in 1951 with a maximum target of 10,000 immigrants per annum to include a proportion of married applicants up to 45 years of age for whom accommodation can be provided by sponsors in New Zealand. All migrants selected in the United Kingdom under these arrangements will now receive free passages.

From 1946 to 1948 the Union of South Africa operated a scheme for State sponsored (but not financially assisted) immigration which succeeded in raising the net movement between the United Kingdom and the Union to some 24,000 during 1948. Since then there has been a heavy decline, and figures for 1950 show a net outward movement from the United Kingdom of only 600.

Southern Rhodesia has absorbed over 60,000 new settlers from all sources in the post-war years. Figures are not available of the total movement to all destinations outside Southern Rhodesia but the Colony's European population has increased from 82,000 in 1946 to approximately 136,000 in 1951, thus imposing a considerable strain on the Colony's economy, accommodation, and social services. Recently the greater part of the inflow has consisted of persons from the Union of South Africa. As from September, 1951, persons in the United Kingdom intending settlement in Southern Rhodesia will be required to obtain a residence permit issued by a Selection Board established in London. Certain applicants who are approved by the Board will receive financial assistance from the Government of Southern Rhodesia in the form of a refund, after arrival in the Colony, of part of the cost of transport.

The cost to the United Kingdom Government of the Government Free Passage Scheme for ex-Service men and women is borne on the vote of the Ministry of Labour and National Service. The Australian Assisted Passage Agreement is operating under the terms of the Empire Settlement Acts, 1922 and 1937, and during the period April, 1947 to October, 1951, contributions were made towards the cost of passages provided for over 90,000 persons.

Total expenditure under the Empire Settlement Acts since the resumption of assisted migration in 1947 and to the end of the financial year 1950-51 has amounted to £2,018,134 (assisted passages to Australia £1,959,005, Child migration £59,129).

During the period January, 1946 to June, 1951, the net migration movement by sea from the United Kingdom to Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa and Southern Rhodesia amounted to approximately 376,000 persons.

CANADA

THE British North America Act (30–31 Vict. Cap. 3) grouped the Provinces of Ontario and Quebec (then constituting the Colony of Canada), New Brunswick and Nova Scotia together in a confederation, to which was given the name of Canada. This Act was brought into force on 1st July, 1867, by Royal Proclamation bearing date the 22nd May, 1867.

The Governor-General of Canada was by Letters Patent of 1st April, 1870, appointed Governor of Rupert's Land. The admission of Rupert's Land into the confederation after the 15th July, 1870, was provided for by Order in Council of the 23rd June, 1870, and the Province of Manitoba was erected out of this Territory by a Canadian Act (33 Vict. cap. 3) and made a Canadian Province on 15th July, 1870.

By Order of Her Majesty in Council dated 16th May, 1871, issued on the authority of the British North America Act 1867, on Addresses from the Houses of Parliament of Canada and the Legislature of British Columbia, that Colony entered the confederation as from the 20th July, 1871.
Prince Edward Island was admitted by Order in Council bearing date 26th June, 1873, on the 1st July in that year.

By an Act passed in 1876 the portion of the North Western Territories lying to the north of Manitoba was created into a separate district, under the name of the District of Keewatin, but under the control of the Lieutenant-Governor of Manitoba.

By Canadian Act 38 Vict. cap. 49 (1875), the territories formerly known as "Rupert's Land" and the North-Western Territory (with the exception of the portion forming the Province of Manitoba) were placed under the jurisdiction and control of a Lieutenant-Governor and Council, separate and distinct from Manitoba.

On the 31st July, 1880, in compliance with the prayer of an Address from the Parliament of Canada dated the 3rd May, 1878, Her Majesty issued an Order in Council annexing to Canada from the 1st September, 1880, such British Possessions in North America (other than Newfoundland) as were not previously included in it.

By Minute of Council of 17th May, 1882, the districts of Assiniboia, Saskatchewan, Alberta, and Athabaska were created, and by Order in Council dated the 2nd October, 1895, modified by Order in Council of 18th December, 1897, the remaining territory was subdivided into the districts of Ungava, Franklin, Mackenzie, and Yukon, the last-named being made a separate territory, distinct from the North-West Territories, under special regulations by chapter 6 of the Acts of the Federal Parliament, 1898.

By Federal Acts of 1905 (Cap. 3 and 42) the four provisional districts of Alberta, Assiniboia, Saskatchewan and Athabaska were formed into the two Provinces of Alberta and Saskatchewan and were admitted into Canada as such on the 1st September, 1905. The dividing line runs north and south.

By Federal Act of 1912, the boundaries of the Provinces of Ontario, Quebec and Manitoba were extended, the whole of Ungava being transferred to Quebec and parts of Keewatin to Ontario and Manitoba. The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council, by the Labrador Boundary Award made on 1st March, 1927, adjudged that an area of about 112,400 square miles, ownership of which had been disputed between Quebec and Newfoundland, was part of the latter.

In 1949 Newfoundland, including Labrador, was joined with the existing nine Provinces as a tenth Province after the people of Newfoundland had by a majority voted in favour in a referendum held in 1948.
For further information about Canada see Canada Year Book .

Situation and Area

Canada occupies the northern half of the American continent (except Alaska, which belongs to the United States) stretching from latitude $49^\circ$ to beyond the polar circle, and including the islands of the northern archipelago lying in the Canadian sector. It is the third largest country in the world, comprising an area computed at 3,848,863 (land and fresh-water) square miles, over forty times the area of the United Kingdom.

According to the 1941 Census, nearly 3½ millions of the people in Canada are of French extraction, mostly residing in Quebec, where French (which, with English, is the official language of the Federal Government of Canada) is generally spoken.

There are about 136,000 Indians in Canada, i.e. , roughly one per cent. of the total population. They are to be found throughout the country, chiefly in the Prairie Provinces (41,000), Ontario (30,000), British Columbia (25,000), and the Northwest Territories. About one-half inhabit the northern and outlying regions, supporting themselves by hunting, fishing, and traffic in furs; the remainder live in Reserves. There were in 1941 34,627 Chinese and 23,149 Japanese in Canada, mostly in British Columbia.
The Eskimaux population of Canada, excluding Labrador, was estimated at 8,437 in 1950, mostly along the northern coasts.
Constitution

The Executive Government is vested in the Crown and is exercised by a Governor-General appointed by the Queen on the recommendation of his Canadian Ministers. The Governor-General exercises his executive powers on the advice of his Cabinet which, as in England, is a committee of the Privy Council, formed of the principal members of the Government and responsible to the Parliament of Canada.

The supreme legislative power in the field of jurisdiction assigned to the federal legislature by the British North America Act is vested in a Parliament, consisting of the Queen (represented by the Governor-General), a Senate, and a House of
Digitized by Google

Commons. The Senate now consists of 102 members nominated for life by the Governor-General and so chosen that 24 represent Ontario, 24 Quebec, 24 the Maritime Provinces, 24 the Western Provinces and (since 31st March, 1949) six Newfoundland. The qualification for Senator is the possession of property worth $4,000, age not less than 30 years, British nationality, and residence within the province for which he is appointed. The House of Commons consisted originally of 181 elected members. This number has been increased by additions on the accession of new provinces and as the result of increase in population, and is at present 262—83 representing Ontario, 73 Quebec, 13 Nova Scotia, 10 New Brunswick, 16 Manitoba, 18 British Columbia, four Prince Edward Island, 17 Alberta, 20 Saskatchewan, seven Newfoundland, and one the Yukon Territory and Mackenzie River. There is no property qualification. A Parliament lasts five years if not sooner dissolved.

For each province there is a Lieutenant-Governor, appointed by the Governor-General in Council and holding office during pleasure, but not removable within five years of appointment except for cause assigned.

Each province has a "Legislative Assembly," and in Quebec there is also a "Legislative Council," forming a second chamber. The Provincial Legislatures possess the power of altering their own constitutions. The territory not comprised within any province (Yukon and the Northwest Territories) is very thinly inhabited. The Yukon Territory is governed by an appointed Commissioner (under instructions from the Governor-General in Council or the Minister of Resources and Development) and an elective legislative council of five members. The Northwest Territories are similarly governed by a Commissioner and eight councillors, of whom three are elected and the rest appointed by the Governor-General in Council.

The Parliament of Canada has exclusive legislative power in certain specified matters and generally in all matters except those specifically assigned by the constitution to the Provincial Legislatures. The Canadian Constitution is in this respect the reverse of that of the United States. Among such matters may be named public finance, trade regulations, postal service, currency, coinage, banking and navigation, defence, the law relating to crimes, bankruptcy, copyright, patents, naturalization, and Indian affairs.

The Provincial Legislatures have powers to legislate in respect of certain specified subjects, of which the chief are property and civil rights, the alteration of their own constitutions, direct taxation within the province and provincial loans, the management of provincial public lands, and of provincial and municipal offices, hospitals, gaols, licences, local works, and the general civil law and procedure. As regards education they have full powers, subject only to certain provisions to secure protection to religious minorities. In agricultural, quarantine and immigration matters they possess concurrent legislative powers with the Parliament of Canada.
The Judges are appointed by the Governor-General in the superior, district, and county courts throughout Canada, except in the probate courts of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick.

By the provisions of the British North America Act and subsequent arrangements entered into from time to time, the Canadian Government is required to make certain annual payments to the individual Provinces. These payments (subsidies) fall into the following classes :—

(A) Interest on Debt Allowances. —By the terms of Confederation in 1867 the Canadian Government assumed all the outstanding debts and liabilities of the Provinces and undertook to pay, except in the case of Ontario and Quebec, interest at 5 per cent. on the amounts by which the actual per capita indebtedness of the Provinces fell short of a basic debt allowance calculated at approximately $25 per capita . On the subsequent entry of additional Provinces into Confederation similar arrangements were effected regarding the assumption of their pre-Confederation indebtedness. From time to time adjustments have been made in
Digitized by Google

the basis of calculation of the debt allowances of the various Provinces and the Canadian Government pays interest at 5 per cent. per annum on the amounts by which the actual debts of the Provinces, on their entry into Confederation, fell short of the allowed debts as adjusted. The aggregate annual payments from federal funds to the Provinces in respect of interest on debt allowances was in 1946 $1,609,386.

(B) Allowances for Government and Legislature. —Under the terms of the Union, annual grants of specific amounts were made to the various Provinces for the support of their Governments and Legislatures. These fixed amounts vary with the population of the Provinces according to the following scale, approved in 1907 :—
Where population is—
The aggregate annual allowances presently paid under this head amount to $1,750,000.

(C) Allowances per Head of Population. —Under the British North America Act of 1867, a grant of 80 cents per head of the population was allowed to each Province. The British North America Act of 1907 provided that the grant would be paid to each Province at the rate of 80 cents per head up to a population of two and one-half million and at the rate of 60 cents per head for so much of the population as exceeds that number. The cost to the Dominion in the fiscal year ended 31st March, 1950, of the annual allowances paid to the Provinces per head of population was $9,206,987.

(D) Special Grants. —In the case of all the Provinces, with the exception of Ontario and Quebec, certain additional grants have been made in view of special circumstances obtaining, which for the fiscal year 1949–50 amounted in aggregate to $2,468,000 as set out below :—
New Brunswick. An annual grant of $150,000 since 1875 in consideration of repeal of lumber duties reserved to the Province by the British North America Act of 1867.
Manitoba. A special grant on the basis of population amounting at present to $562,500 per annum.
Prince Edward Island. A special grant of $195,000 less a deduction of $39,120.10.
British Columbia. A special grant of $100,000 per annum.

Saskatchewan and Alberta are currently receiving an annual sum as compensation for loss of revenue derivable from their Public Lands, based on their respective populations, which amounts in the case of Saskatchewan to $750,000, and in the case of Alberta to $750,000.

The British North America Act, 1867, provided that Bills passed by the Provincial Legislatures require the assent of the Lieutenant-Governor and may be disallowed within a year by the Governor-General, and those passed by the Dominion Parliament require the assent of the Governor-General and may be disallowed within two years by the Queen.

Provision was made in the Act constituting the Dominion for the admission into it of any of the other British possessions on the continent of North America, and they have all since been admitted. Provision was also made for the construction of a railway from the River St. Lawrence to Halifax and (in the Order in Council admitting British Columbia) for the construction of a railway from the Atlantic to the Pacific Ocean. Both these have been constructed.

THE PROVINCES OLD CANADA (QUEBEC AND ONTARIO)
History

The Atlantic coast was discovered by Leif Ericson about 1000 A.D. and re-discovered by Sebastion Cabot in 1497. Jacquest Cartier landed at Gaspé in 1534 and sailed on to discover the St. Lawrence River. From 1603 onwards Samuel de Champlain explored the Atlantic seaboard, founding Port Royal (Nova Scotia) and Quebec (1608), and finally pushed up the Ottawa River to the upper Great Lakes. French explorers subsequently travelled the continent from Hudson Bay to the mouth of the Mississippi and from the Saguenay River to the Rockies.

Until 1663 Quebec was governed autocratically by a trading company; in that year it became a Royal Province, under a Governor to whom as the personal representative of the King of France were entrusted the general policy of the country, the direction of its military affairs, and its relations with the Indian tribes. The Bishop, as head of the Church, was supreme in matters affecting religion, and the Intendant, acting under the authority of the King, not of the Governor, was responsible for the administration of justice, for finance, and for the direction of local administration. A Superior Council also existed, with certain administrative powers more formal than real. This system continued until the end of the French régime.
In 1759 Quebec succumbed to British forces under General Wolfe, and in 1763 the whole territory of Canada was ceded to Great Britain by the Treaty of Paris.

From 1763 to 1774 Canada was governed in the main by military authority. In the latter year the Quebec Act, passed by the Parliament of Great Britain, secured for the French colonists the right to retain their language, religion, and civil law. Roman Catholics were allowed the free exercise of their religion and the Catholic clergy were declared entitled to receive their accustomed dues and rights with respect to those professing the Catholic religion: and in civil cases it was declared that resort should be had to the Laws of Canada (i.e., the old French civil laws) for the decision of the same. A nominee Council was also established. The boundaries were extended to the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, but were withdrawn to the Great Lakes in 1783.

In 1791 an Act of Parliament was passed (31 Geo. III, cap. 31), authorizing the Crown to issue a proclamation dividing Canada into two Provinces, Upper Canada (now Ontario), in which many loyalists from the American colonies had settled, and Lower Canada (now Quebec), and establishing a constitution for each. The first step towards democratic institutions was taken by the establishment of elected Houses of Assembly, though the Governors, appointed by the Crown and the nominated legislative councils, retained control. This resulted in increasing difficulties, especially in Lower Canada, between the Houses of Assembly and the Executive Governments after the removal of immediate danger with the end of the American War in 1814, and they continued until the risings of 1837-38, which resulted in the suspension of the constitution of Lower Canada and the appointment of Lord Durham as Governor-General with special powers and instructions to report on the affairs of the country. He recommended effective control of the executive by the elected representatives of the people, i.e., responsible as distinct from representative government, and to this end the immediate union of Upper and Lower Canada and the ultimate union of all British North America. Upper and Lower Canada were accordingly reunited by Act 3 and 4 Vic. cap. 35, and the legislative bodies were consolidated. The new House of Assembly, created in 1840, consisted of 84 members, there being an equal number from each Province. The new Legislative Council was to consist of not less than 20 members (it subsequently exceeded double that number), and the members were appointed for life. Subsequently (by 17 and 18 Vict. Cap. 118) the Canadian Legislature was empowered to alter the Constitution of the Legislative Council, and this power was utilized in 1856 by an Act of Canada which made half of the Legislative Council elective for terms of eight years. The modification was an important one, for it was because of the dissatisfaction caused by the behaviour of certain elected Councillors that, on
Digitized by Google
the creation of the Dominion of Canada in 1867, the new Senate was made appointive.
Municipal Institutions were established in Canada in 1841.

As years passed, increasing difficulty was experienced in the conduct of public affairs owing to the division of interest of Upper and Lower Canada, while the development of communications and the outbreak of the Civil War in the United States emphasized both the possibility and the desirability of confederation. Negotiations resulted in agreement between Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick and the passage of the British North America Act, 1867.
ONTARIO
Situation and Population

The Province stretches 1,000 miles from east to west, from Quebec to the Prairies, and slightly more than 1,000 miles from south to north, from the Great Lakes to Hudson Bay. Its area is 412,582 sq. miles, and the population in 1949 was estimated at 4,411,000, about one-third of the total population of Canada. The capital city is Toronto, the population of which, according to the preliminary figure of the 1951 Census, is 670,945, and greater Toronto has a population of over 1,100,000. About 72 per cent. of the population are of British stock, and almost 10 per cent. are French-speaking.
Constitution
(For development prior to 1867 see under "Old Canada.")

The House of Assembly is elected for five years on an adult suffrage basis, with no property qualification. It consists of 90 members. At the last election, in 1951, the Progressive Conservative Party gained a majority of 66 seats, the Liberal Party securing 8, the Co-operative Commonwealth Federation 2, Liberal Labour 1, and Labour Progressive 1.
Lieutenant-Governor : The Hon. Ray Lawson, O.B.E., LL.D.

QUEBEC
Situation and Population

Quebec lies on both sides of the Lower St. Lawrence and extends from the New England states of the U.S.A. to the Davis Straits. Its area is 594,860 square miles. The population in 1949 was estimated at 3,887,000, of whom, according to the preliminary figure of the 1951 Census, 1,370,444 live in Greater Montreal, the world's largest inland port, which is 1,000 miles up the St. Lawrence River from the Atlantic Ocean. The capital city is Quebec with a population of 161,439.

NOVA SCOTIA
Situation and Population

Nova Scotia consists of the peninsula of Nova Scotia and the island of Cape Breton, both lying between the Gulf of St. Lawrence and the Atlantic Ocean. The area is 21,068 square miles. The population in 1949 was estimated at 645,000, of whom, according to the preliminary figure of the 1951 Census, 84,433 live in the capital city, Halifax.
History and Constitutional Development

Nova Scotia was first discovered by the Norsemen and rediscovered by John Cabot in 1497 ; it was colonized by the French in 1598 ; was taken by the English, and a grant of it made to Sir W. Alexander by James I in 1621. In 1632 it was restored to France, with Quebec, by the Treaty of St. Germain-en-laye, but again ceded to England at the Peace of Utrecht in 1714. After the Peace of Aix-la-Chapelle in 1748, a settlement of disbanded troops was formed there by Lord Halifax, and the city which now bears his name is the capital of the province. Cape Breton was not finally taken from the French until 1758, in which year the first Assembly was summoned. Some 35,000 loyalists moved to Nova Scotia from the former American colonies to the south when the independence of the latter was recognized in 1783 and the last British troops withdrawn. In 1769 Prince Edward Island became a separate colony and in 1784 New Brunswick and Cape
Digitized by Google

Breton Island were also separated from the rest of Nova Scotia to which Cape Breton Island was later reunited. In 1848 responsible Government was established, and in 1867 Nova Scotia was one of the four colonies which united to form Canada, of which it became a Province.
Constitution

The legislature now consists of a single House, the House of Assembly of 37 members, elected for five years. The present Government is formed from the Liberal Party which secured a majority of 17 seats at the last election in 1949. The Progressive Party secured 8 seats and the Co-operative Commonwealth Federation Party 2.

NEW BRUNSWICK
Situation and Population

New Brunswick is one of the Maritime Provinces of Canada and consists of the mainland between Quebec and Nova Scotia. The area of the Province is 27,985 square miles, and the population in 1949 was estimated at 516,000. The provincial capital is Fredericton with a population of 15,870, according to the preliminary figure of the 1951 Census.
History and Constitutional Development

New Brunswick was part of the ancient French Province of Acadia and was ceded to England by the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713. Great Britain, however, did not obtain full possession of the country until after the fall of Quebec in 1759. It was first colonized by British subjects from New England in 1762, and in 1783, at the close of the Revolutionary War, it received a large body of Loyalists from the Thirteen Colonies. In 1784 it was separated from Nova Scotia, of which it had formed a part, and given a separate Governor and Assembly. The colony remained quiet and prosperous, largely free from the conflicts between Executive and Legislature which vexed the other North American colonies, until 1837. In 1854 responsible Government was established, and in 1867 New Brunswick was one of the colonies which agreed to form Canada, of which it became an original Province.
Constitution

The legislature consists of a single House, the Legislative Assembly, of 52 members. The present Government is formed from the Liberal Party, which at the election in 1948 secured a majority of 42 seats over the Progressive Conservatives.

MANITOBA
Situation and Population

Manitoba was the first to be formed of the Prairie Provinces, and when created in 1870 included only a small area south of Lake Winnipeg. In 1912 it was increased to its present size of 246,512 square miles. The population of the Province in 1949 was estimated at 778,000, of whom Winnipeg, the provincial capital, has 233,617, according to the preliminary figure of the 1951 Census.
Constitution

Manitoba was formed from part of Rupert's Land, the territory granted by Charles II to Prince Rupert in 1671. It became a Province of the Canadian Federation by legislative enactments taking effect on the 15th July, 1870.

The legislature consists of a single House, the Legislative Assembly of 58 members, elected for five years. At the election in 1949 the Coalition secured a majority of 29 seats (Liberal-Progressives 30, Progressive Conservatives 9, Independents 4); the Anti-Coalition secured 14 seats (Co-operative Commonwealth Federation 7, Independent 6 and Labour Progressive one).

BRITISH COLUMBIA
Situation and Population

British Columbia is the westernmost Province of Canada and lies between the Rocky Mountains and the Pacific Ocean. The area (including Vancouver Island and Queen Charlotte Islands) is 366,255 square miles. The population in 1949 was estimated at 817,861, of whom, according to the preliminary figures of the 1951 Census, 340,272 live in Vancouver and 50,774 in the capital, Victoria.
History and Constitution

Vancouver Island was granted to the Hudson's Bay Company under certain conditions in 1849, and in the same year it was made a Crown colony. The mainland of British Columbia was constituted a Crown colony in 1858, in consequence of large immigration on the discovery of gold in the Thompson and Fraser Rivers. In 1866 the two colonies of British Columbia and Vancouver Island were united under the former name, and in 1871 British Columbia became a Province of Canada.
